title: Hello :)
text:
I am a computer vision researcher and biomedical engineer with expertise in developing multimodal models and finetuning foundation models for on-edge deployment on robots, and in clinical settings. I am currently a core ML team member at the DARPA Triage Challenge, developing multimodal deep learning models for battlefield trauma detection. The bulk of my work at CMU has centered around this role - rapid testing and deployment of algorithms for physiological sign sensing using robots. Given our aggressive deployment-first approach, I have more cool videos to show than NeurIPS publications - see them here.
Aside from my work on multimodal algorithms for medical triage robotics, I have worked on the below topics:
- Machine Unlearning for privacy-preserving AI
- Imitation Learning for long-horizon task planning in embodied AI simulations
- Uncertainty-aware segmentation models for tumor and pleural effusion segmentation
📌 Check out my Projects!!
✍️ For my thoughts on AI & research, visit the Blog.
📄 My resume (PDF)





J on the rocks.

title: On the Evolution of Research Language:
text: “The language. Over time you’ll develop a vocabulary of good words and bad words to use when writing papers. Speaking about machine learning or computer vision papers specifically as concrete examples, in your papers you never “study” or “investigate” (there are boring, passive, bad words); instead you “develop” or even better you “propose”. And you don’t present a “system” or, shudder, a “pipeline”; instead, you develop a “model”. You don’t learn “features”, you learn “representations”. And god forbid, you never “combine”, “modify” or “expand”. These are incremental, gross terms that will certainly get your paper rejected :)” - said karpathy in his 2016 blog. As a new PhD student, Im curious about how this has changed. Can I run some experiments to answer this?

title: Concepts & Exposition
text:
Take a quick example and let’s see how TD Learning works.
Take a simple linear track path with 4 states - S0, S1, S2 and a final “treasure” state.
Goal: Learn the “value” of being in each state.
Rules: - Agent starts at S0. - Episode ends when treasure is reached. - Treasure gets you +10 reward. - Every other step gets a reward of 0. - All states start with a value of 0; the agent is unaware of the presence of a treasure at the end.

import numpy as np
import time
from IPython.display import clear_output


num_states = 3
reward_val = 10.0

V = np.zeros(num_states)

lr = 0.1
gamma = 0.9 #discount factor

num_episodes = 50


# Learning loop

print("Bootstrapping Process Exposition")
print("Agent starts with V =", V, "\n")
time.sleep(3)

for i in range(num_episodes):
    for s in range(num_states):
        s_next = s+1
        # the values that are important will be present (s) and next (s+1)

        if s_next == num_states:
            value_next = 0 #reaching treasure is terminal.
            R = reward_val
        else:
            R = 0
            value_next = V[s_next]

        # core TD Learning update
        # TD Target = r + γ*V(s')
        target = R + gamma*value_next
        
        # get the TD error (delta)
        delta = target - V[s]
            
        # calculate the update to current V[s]
        V[s] = V[s] + lr * delta
        
    #print the intermediate values; to see how the agent learns.
    if i+1 in [1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 49]:
        clear_output(wait=True)
        print(f"--- After Episode {i+1} ---")
        print("Agent's current knowledge:")
        print(f"V(S₀) = {V[0]:.2f}")
        print(f"V(S₁) = {V[1]:.2f}")
        print(f"V(S₂) = {V[2]:.2f}")
        time.sleep(2)

clear_output(wait=True)
print("--- Final Learned Values ---")
print(f"V(S₀) = {V[0]:.2f}")
print(f"V(S₁) = {V[1]:.2f}")
print(f"V(S₂) = {V[2]:.2f}")
print("\n--- 'Ground Truth' Values ---")
print("V(S₀) = 0.9 * 8.1  = 7.29")
print("V(S₁) = 0.9 * 9.0  = 8.10")
print("V(S₂) = 0.9 * 10   = 9.00")

--- Final Learned Values ---
V(S₀) = 7.19
V(S₁) = 8.70
V(S₂) = 9.95

--- 'Ground Truth' Values ---
V(S₂) = 0.9 * 10   = 9.00
V(S₁) = 0.9 * 9.0  = 8.10
V(S₀) = 0.9 * 8.1  = 7.29


let’s plot the curve of how these values was learned!

title: On Grounding
text:
Why this post? Well - as a computer vision researcher working in health sensing, I stumbled into the concept of grounding out of sheer necessity. Without revealing too much, I’ll just say that my grant required me to solve a tough medical problem where both false positives and false negatives were heavily penalized. The naive vision model I used had a tendency towards false positives, and I soon discovered that I could used visually grounded models to reduce the tendency for false positives by bringing in a granular ‘world knowledge’.
with all this hype around grounding; and as someone who merely chanced into it in the process of solving a pressing deployment problem, I feel compelled to understsnd this concept. It’s fascinating how, for example G-DINO is able to do this, and I want to understand this as deeply as I can.

title: Visual Reasoning
text:
Jul 3, 2025
Well - this is the way it started - started on the ARC AGI challenge - and on the 2025 challenge, we see that the best models are at 4% accuracy given the current budget.
Reading more, you start seeing people literally fighting it out. The illusion of thinking paper from Apple claims (as the title would have you think) that it’s an illusion that models think - that they have an accuracy collapse once task complexity exceeds a certain threshold. Then a paper came flying back out, the ‘Illusion of the illusion of thinking’, funnily authored by Claude and Lawsen - likely a pen name - that takes no prisoners in its criticism of the paper, calling its automated evaluation mechanism entirely flawed - and that by that very token the paper exposes the importance of good engineering in AI research - and that it doesn’t do much else.
https://www.reddit.com/r/datascience/comments/1ld06j0/the_illusion_of_the_illusion_of_thinking/ Turns out that this is a BIG debate!
https://lawsen.substack.com/p/when-your-joke-paper-goes-viral
a more detailed take:
https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/5uw26uDdFbFQgKzih/beware-general-claims-about-generalizable-reasoning
A joke paper, it looks like.
On a more serious note:
Quite a few works indicate that all of higher human cognition is compositional.
And there’s works that point to the limits of compositionality in tranformers. Combining these two, does it then become obvious that transformer based models - in all their hyper scale glory - will not be able to reach a state that can be termed human-level cognition?
(worth digging into the details of how the limits of compositionality were explored in that paper (Faith and Fate). Also pull in the other compositionality papers and cross-reason.).
(thread 2 - is compositionality the only keyword? Or are there other components as well?)
“many of the issues pointed to in earlier stochastic parrot papers seem to have been mitigated with increasing model scale”.
“The authors also do some statistical analysis of the model generated chains-of-thought (CoTs). From this, they first find that “models counterintuitively begin to reduce their reasoning effort despite increasing problem difficulty”. They also find that in the Tower of Hanoi case, “counterintuitively”, providing the correct algorithm to the model does not seem to improve performance. ”
I think that it’s intuitive though - the same way I lose traction when faced with a leetcode hard problem now (me working through leetcodes every morning could be a good exercise in grasping how I am learning to reason - my reasoning is highly spatial). There were also studies saying that kids good at spatial reasoning are good at math no? Yeah - so is it that models trained on more spatial challenges might become better at math?
what experiments can I setup to test the above??

title: Multi TimeScale RL
text: Inspired by the Nature paper, I want to setup some experiments to see how much these ideas really apply to a real RL case.

title: Multi TimeScale RL
text:
The leap to multi-timescale RL
The authors propose that the brain overcomes this “value confounding” issue by having a spectrum of patience in its dopamine responder population. “Imagine a population of dopamine neurons, where each neuron (or group of neurons) has its own intrinsic gamma” (is this correct??)
Instead of a single scalar value V; the agent now computes a vector of values - one for each timescale.
V_vector = [ R * γ1t, R * γ2t, R * γ3t, … ]

def plot_multi_timescale_signature(R1=5, t1=3, R2=8, t2=8):
    """
    Calculates and plots the value "signature" for two scenarios
    across a range of discount factors.
    """
    # A vector of different gammas, from impatient (0.1) to patient (0.99)
    gammas = np.linspace(0.4, 0.99, 100)

    # Calculate the value for each scenario across ALL gammas
    V_vector1 = R1 * (gammas ** t1)
    V_vector2 = R2 * (gammas ** t2)

    # Plotting
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))
    ax.plot(gammas, V_vector1, label=f'Scenario 1 (R={R1}, t={t1})', lw=3)
    ax.plot(gammas, V_vector2, label=f'Scenario 2 (R={R2}, t={t2})', lw=3)

    ax.set_xlabel('Discount Factor (γ)', fontsize=14)
    ax.set_ylabel('Calculated Value (V)', fontsize=14)
    ax.set_title('Multi-Timescale Value "Signatures"', fontsize=16)
    ax.legend(fontsize=12)
    ax.grid(True, which='both', linestyle='--', linewidth=0.5)
    plt.show()

import pdb; pdb.set_trace()
# Create an interactive widget
interact(
    plot_multi_timescale_signature,
    R1=IntSlider(min=1, max=20, step=1, value=5, description='Reward 1'),
    t1=IntSlider(min=1, max=20, step=1, value=3, description='Delay 1'),
    R2=IntSlider(min=1, max=20, step=1, value=8, description='Reward 2'),
    t2=IntSlider(min=1, max=20, step=1, value=8, description='Delay 2')
)

---> 25 import pdb; pdb.set_trace()

--KeyboardInterrupt--

KeyboardInterrupt: Interrupted by user





This is now really interesting - instead of having the same singular value for a Reward - Time combination; the array of gammas allows for the generation of a nice curve!! This then allows for a nice characterization of the situation!! For example here - the small but immediate reward stars higher, but crosses over, and in the “patient POV”, is as a much lower value; whereas the high, late reward is valued lower; and sharply grows in value. Interesting to think about how maybe people with addiction might be attuned to prefer situations with the blue curve over the orange curve.

Disentangling Time and Reward

title: History of MCMC
text: Why write about this?? Well - even modern techniques like the work by Yilun Du on compositional generalization using diffusion models seems to use foundational principles from MCMC. Curious, started digging into the stories of how this came about - as in, when and why did researchers even start digging into the whole “I want to draw samples from a distribution, regardless of how intractable getting an explicit representation of the distribution is”? Found this from Gemini - but ig might be worth doing my own reading and going into the history of this.

title: Projects
text:
Development and Deployment of Remote Health Sensing Models

“When we have a significant emergency where there could be danger to the responders, you can save lives by not sending in a human right away. This autonomous triage system allows us to provide rapid help to as many victims as possible without needing extra human power — an essential gap to fill when medics are limited.”
— Dr. Lenny Weiss, Professor of Emergency Medicine, UPitt; Medical Director of City of Pittsburgh Police-SWAT

 







Developed an ARKit-based system to assess ocular alertness via blink rate tracking. 





Led testing across classical spectral-reflectance models and transformer-based architectures.
Final model deployed: a 3D CNN-based system, benchmarked extensively on custom IRB-approved datasets.

📹 Demo
  




Conducted extensive benchmarking of segmentation models and Vision-Language Models (VLMs).
Final deployment: A segmentation model detecting severe hemorrhage presence in real-time robotic data streams.

📹 Live Robotic Data Demo
   

This research opened a new problem space—achieving grounding in small models for data-scarce tasks.
As we tackle the much harder challenge of performing hemorrhage detection in further degraded sensing environments for Year #2 Challenge (daytime operations in thick smoke, nightime operations with no visible illumination, etc.) we are continuing to investigate - (i) how do we achieve strong visual grounding in unstructured environments despite the scarcity of relevant data? and (ii) how do we use multimodal data streams - like multispectral imaging - to characterize hemorrhage presence even in absence of RGB illumination?

title: Hi there!
text: In a constant state of figuring. I’m in my element when I have to make things work in high-stakes settings. Love writing as an exercise in thought and imagination. As for leisure - I love to make time for my friends, cook, talk outlandish philosphy, and go climbing or lifting.
